name: Parque Natural Babia y Luna from Murias de Paredes – circular through Babia Biosphere Reserve
name_original: Parque Natural Babia y Luna desde Murias de Paredes – circular por Reserva de la Biosfera de Babia
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Murias De Paredes traverse the UNESCO Universal Biosphere Reserve in León, Spain, offering varied terrain within the Cantabrian Mountains. The landscape features peaks exceeding 2,000 meters, extensive forests of oak, ash, and birch, and the upper reaches of the Omaña River. Cyclists encounter both demanding climbs and more gentle sections, making the region suitable for diverse skill levels. This area is characterized by its natural beauty and tranquil environment, providing a focused road cycling experience. Best road cycling routes around Murias De Paredes The most popular road cycling route is Parque Natural Babia y Luna from Murias de Paredes – circular through Babia Biosphere Reserve, a 68.9 miles (110.8 km) trail that takes 5 hours 7 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through a protected natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is View of Villablino – Albergue Leitariegos loop from Villablino, a difficult 53.0 miles (85.3 km) path. This route includes substantial climbing, offering panoramic views of the surrounding mountainous terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Albergue Leitariegos – View of Villablino loop from Robles de Laciana, a 26.9 miles (43.3 km) trail leading through the mountainous region, often completed in about 2 hours 19 minutes. Road cycling around Murias De Paredes is defined by the Cantabrian Mountains, extensive forests, and varied terrain including challenging climbs and scenic river valleys. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Murias De Paredes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 350 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Murias De Paredes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty can I expect on road cycling routes in Murias De Paredes?
A: Murias De Paredes, nestled in the Cantabrian Mountains, offers varied terrain for road cycling. You'll find both demanding climbs, with some peaks exceeding 2,000 meters, and more gentle sections. The region has 11 moderate and 16 difficult routes, catering to cyclists seeking a challenge as well as those looking for a less strenuous ride. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042321544/parque-natural-babia-y-luna-from-murias-de-paredes-circular-through-babia-biosphere-reserve" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque Natural Babia y Luna from Murias de Paredes&lt;/a&gt; feature significant elevation gain through protected natural parks.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I expect to see while road cycling in Murias De Paredes?
A: While road cycling in Murias De Paredes, you'll traverse a UNESCO Universal Biosphere Reserve, characterized by extensive forests of oak, ash, holly, and Europe's largest mass of birch trees. The landscape includes glacial-shaped mountains and ecologically valuable lagoons like Montrondo, Torrecillo, and Vegapujín. You might also encounter beautiful waterfalls such as Cascada de Lumajo and Cascada Pozo Los Fumos, or historical elements like ancient castros and stone bridges.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints or attractions worth visiting along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, several attractions enhance the cycling experience. You can find historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2611232" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palace of Quiñones&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5518472" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Campo de Martín Moro Toledano&lt;/a&gt;. For stunning views, routes often pass by significant peaks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5555072" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Nevadín Summit (2,077 m)&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5518203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Catoute (2,117 m)&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5506458" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ponferrada–Villablino Railway Greenway&lt;/a&gt; also offers a unique cycling path.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Murias De Paredes?
A: The region's natural beauty, with lush forests and varied terrain, makes it appealing across seasons. However, given the presence of mountains and potential for demanding climbs, spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for road cycling. Summers can be warm, while winters might bring colder conditions and snow at higher elevations.

Q: Are there options for less experienced road cyclists in Murias De Paredes?
A: While many routes in Murias De Paredes are classified as difficult, there are also 11 moderate routes available. These routes offer a less strenuous experience while still allowing you to enjoy the region's beautiful landscapes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31849462" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albergue Leitariegos – View of Villablino loop from Robles de Laciana&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 43.3 km trail that can be completed in about 2 hours 19 minutes.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Murias De Paredes?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes available around Murias De Paredes, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. These routes explore the diverse landscapes of the UNESCO Universal Biosphere Reserve, from challenging mountain passes to scenic river valleys.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Murias De Paredes?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the tranquil environment, the stunning natural landscapes, and the varied terrain that provides both challenging climbs and picturesque, quiet secondary roads. The opportunity to cycle through extensive forests and past ecologically valuable lagoons is frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, Murias De Paredes offers several long-distance road cycling routes for those seeking extended rides. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042321544/parque-natural-babia-y-luna-from-murias-de-paredes-circular-through-babia-biosphere-reserve" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque Natural Babia y Luna from Murias de Paredes&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 110.8 km circular route, perfect for a full day of cycling through a protected natural park.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Murias De Paredes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end your ride in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38276671" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Villablino – Albergue Leitariegos loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a challenging circular path with panoramic views.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Murias De Paredes?
A: While specific designated cyclist parking areas are not detailed, Murias De Paredes is a municipality with various starting points for routes. It is generally possible to find parking in or near the villages and towns that serve as trailheads for the cycling routes, such as Murias de Paredes itself or Villablino.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while cycling in the region?
A: As Murias De Paredes is part of a UNESCO Universal Biosphere Reserve, the region is rich in biodiversity. While cycling, especially in quieter areas or near the sources of the Omaña and Valle Gordo rivers, you might have the rare opportunity to spot diverse wildlife, including brown bears and Cantabrian capercaillie.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural points of interest along the cycling routes?
A: Beyond natural beauty, the region boasts a rich history. You can find evidence of Bronze Age settlements and Roman occupation, including canals and tunnels. Traditional architecture, ancient castros like El Cubichón, and historical religious sites such as the 18th-century hermitage of Barrio de la Puente or the pre-Romanesque hermitage of Puerto de La Magdalena, offer glimpses into the area's past.
tips: The Bebé y Luna Natural Park is a unique and valuable diverse natural ecosystem with its impressive limestone massifs of the Cantabrian mountain range. A spectacular landscape for adventure.

Barrios de Luna Reservoir that was built to take advantage of the water of the Luna River to produce electricity. The Reservoir made famous by the battle of the residence of the town of Miñera de Luna to stop the construction and prevent their houses from disappearing under water like many other towns in Spain. In dry years, you can watch the ghost town rise from below.
Ruins of the medieval military strategic Castillo de Luna overlooking the Luna dam. Spectacular structure like something from a fantasy story.
Los Barrios de Luna is a small town in the shadow of the Luna Dam that retains the waters of the Luna River in this incredibly beautiful valley.

Flanked by some of the best-preserved birch forests in the entire region, this town is the head of a municipality that includes fourteen towns spread over three valleys, Omaña and the Chico and Gordo valleys. It is one of those areas that has suffered hard from the scourge of depopulation, but once you are here you still want to change the statistics and stay to live.
